Principle: grounded generation across all types

Every generated text of every type is grounded in a program-generated fact set: the program authors the logic and the LLM only writes it up in the requested register. Rationale: a small model can only learn patterns that are actually there; text whose internal logic is loose or contradictory gives it nothing stable to learn.

Each prompt embeds a grounding from worldgen (a consistent world fragment, or a transfer, ratio, or order puzzle with a program-derived answer and derivation) and instructs the LLM to weave the facts in naturally without contradicting them, and, where a problem is worked, to reach the given correct answer rather than compute its own. The same grounding machinery serves prose, conversation, definition, description, reasoning, and the instruction pairs, so a mathematical puzzle can surface as a dialogue, a description, or a worked solution.

This principle was adopted after the first full-rung results showed fluent text with unstable semantics (a model that could not hold a referent constant across a sentence), on the argument that mixing grounded and ungrounded generation would leave a large share of the corpus logically loose and dilute the learnable signal, so grounding is applied to all types at once, for now at least. Instruction pairs additionally require the user turn to state the facts, making every pair answerable from its own context, which trains exactly the in-context binding that the evaluation probe (node 33) measures. Construction-solving asymmetry (node 37) is what makes this cheap: the program runs the easy forward direction (sampling ranks, ratios, transfers) and supplies both the hard-direction question and its answer to the writer, so the generator LLM never needs to solve anything. The renderer is trusted for now; round-trip verification of the rendered text against the grounding (node 38) is the next safeguard, not yet implemented.
